title: Adopt direct user-bin install and release verification conventions
description:
Goal
Update the standalone-only redesign so install behaves like a normal CLI installer and has a credible release-integrity story. This refines the installer task before implementation.

Current convention research
Well-regarded curl installers generally install a standalone executable into a user bin path or a tool-owned bin path, with an override:
- uv documents a standalone installer and removes binaries from ~/.local/bin on uninstall.
- mise defaults MISE_INSTALL_PATH to ~/.local/bin/mise and supports overriding the binary path.
- Deno installs to a tool-owned bin dir configurable via DENO_INSTALL.
- rustup remains the classic curl | sh flow with TLS flags and interactive guidance.

Decision for br-orphanage
- Install the executable directly as br-orphanage in a user binary path, preferably ~/.local/bin/br-orphanage by default.
- Support an override such as BR_ORPHANAGE_INSTALL_PATH=/some/path/br-orphanage.
- Do not install any br symlink, shadow shim, shell-intercept hook, or passthrough mechanism.
- If ~/.local/bin is missing, create it when reasonable.
- If the chosen install dir is not on PATH, print focused PATH guidance for br-orphanage only.
- Keep data/cache state separate from the executable path; only executable installation belongs in ~/.local/bin.

Dev workflow implication
Once stable installs do not manipulate br, PATH manipulation can belong exclusively to local development:
- Stable: br-orphanage resolves to ~/.local/bin/br-orphanage or the chosen install path.
- Dev: prepend a repo/dev bin path, or run ./bin/br-orphanage directly, so br-orphanage resolves to the working tree.
- Never change what br means in either stable or dev mode.

Security and release-integrity requirements
- Release assets should include the br-orphanage executable and a SHA256 checksum file.
- The installer should verify the downloaded executable against its checksum when installing from a release asset. This protects against corrupt downloads or asset mismatch, but not against a compromised repo/release account if the checksum is replaced too.
- Document a cautious install path: download install.sh, inspect it, then run it.
- Add a follow-up release-hardening path for GitHub artifact attestations or Sigstore/cosign verification. These provide a stronger independent provenance signal than a same-origin checksum.
- Prefer GitHub artifact attestations for this project once release automation exists, because users can verify provenance with gh attestation verify ... -R happycollision/br-orphanage.
- Consider immutable GitHub releases once release assets are attached, so published assets cannot be silently replaced.

Implementation relationship
This task should be completed before the installer rewrite task. The installer rewrite should absorb this decision instead of preserving the current ~/.local/share/br-orphanage/bin plus PATH symlink model.

Acceptance
- README install section says the normal install target is ~/.local/bin/br-orphanage and documents BR_ORPHANAGE_INSTALL_PATH or the chosen override variable.
- install.sh installs the executable directly at the selected install path.
- install.sh output contains no br shadowing language and no tool-owned bin shim unless explicitly justified.
- tests cover default install path, override install path, PATH guidance when install dir is not on PATH, and absence of any br symlink.
- Release/security docs explain the limit of checksums and name attestations/signatures as the stronger protection against distribution compromise.
close_reason: Adopted direct user-bin install path, override, and release-integrity documentation

title: Investigate wrapper PATH shadowing: real br wins in some shells
description:
While adding issues via Claude Code (2026-07-03), plain 'br orphanage sync' failed with "unrecognized subcommand 'orphanage'" because the shell resolved br to the real binary, not the wrapper.

Observed on this machine (macOS, zsh):
- 'which br' in a non-interactive shell -> /Users/don.denton/.local/bin/br (the real beads_rust binary)
- The wrapper is correctly installed at ~/.local/share/br-orphanage/bin/br
- Workaround used: invoke the wrapper by full path (~/.local/share/br-orphanage/bin/br orphanage sync), which worked fine

Root cause hypothesis: install.sh adds the wrapper dir to PATH via a marked line in ~/.bashrc / ~/.zshrc, but (a) non-interactive shells (scripts, agent tool calls, cron, CI) don't source those rc files, and/or (b) even when sourced, the line may append the wrapper dir after ~/.local/bin instead of prepending it, so the real br still wins.

Things to investigate / decide:
1. Check the actual PATH ordering in an interactive shell on this machine — is the wrapper actually shadowing br there, or is it silently broken everywhere?
2. Should install.sh prepend the wrapper dir (and verify it lands ahead of common bin dirs like ~/.local/bin, homebrew, cargo)?
3. Should install.sh warn at install time if 'command -v br' after the PATH edit still doesn't resolve to the wrapper?
4. Consider non-interactive coverage: e.g. document the full-path invocation, or add ~/.zshenv support, or install the wrapper as a symlink earlier in an already-first dir.
5. 'br orphanage --version' could double as a health check: a doctor-style check that warns when the wrapper is installed but not first on PATH.
design:
Root cause: install.sh wrote the PATH-prepend line only to ~/.bashrc and ~/.zshrc. zsh sources ~/.zshrc for INTERACTIVE shells only; non-interactive zsh (agent tool calls, scripts, cron, CI) sources ~/.zshenv, which had no marker -> real br at ~/.local/bin shadowed the wrapper. Hypothesis (b) 'wrong ordering' was disproved: the line correctly prepends (wrapper is #1 on PATH in interactive login zsh). Only hypothesis (a) held.

Fix (install.sh): target ~/.zshenv for zsh (the file zsh sources for EVERY invocation), creating it when the user uses zsh (existing ~/.zshenv/~/.zshrc, or $SHELL=zsh). bash-only users get no ~/.zshenv. Interactive zsh keeps working (it sources ~/.zshenv before ~/.zshrc).

Test (tests/run.sh): new section 'PATH shadowing' asserts a NON-interactive zsh (env -i, clean PATH with real br present but wrapper dir absent) resolves br to the installed wrapper, plus an interactive regression guard. Verified RED before the fix, GREEN after. Full suite 142/142.
notes: Follow-on (same session): extended install.sh to ALWAYS print setup guidance — which startup files it configured (or that it configured none), a note naming the user's shell when it's not bash/zsh, the exact 'export PATH' line, verify commands, and an always-works full-path fallback (~/.local/share/br-orphanage/bin/br ...). bash coverage is partial by nature: interactive bash via ~/.bashrc, but non-interactive non-login bash has no universally-sourced file (only $BASH_ENV, chicken-and-egg) — hence the printed fallback. Other shells (fish, nushell) likewise fall to the printed guidance. README Install section corrected (~/.zshrc -> ~/.zshenv) with a new 'Shell PATH setup' subsection. 2 new TDD tests; full suite 149/149.
close_reason: Fixed: install.sh now writes the PATH line to ~/.zshenv so non-interactive zsh resolves the wrapper. TDD regression test added; full suite 142/142.

title: Design: git-nook — git for a hidden directory
description:
# git-nook: Git for a Hidden Directory

**Status: DRAFT — revised 2026-07-06 (named `git nook`; inner-repo
architecture; no sync command), awaiting design approval.**
This file is a working artifact tracked as plain markdown on this working
branch (per the AGENTS.md branch override — NOT converted to beads for this
effort). The design/plan commits are removed from history before this branch
merges to master.

## Motivation

Some files someone would *like* to track cannot be tracked in the main repo
(for whatever reason). This tool gives them **real git tracking** — status,
commits, diffs, log, merges, conflicts, push/pull — inside a directory the
parent repo does not see, published to a custom ref (`refs/nook/...`) that
never appears in branch listings, host UIs, or default clones.

The tool is a **channel creator plus a git shim**. It orchestrates nothing:
once a nook exists, every operation is a plain git command everyone already
knows.

## Core architecture: a nook is a hidden inner git repository

```
.git/nook/notes.git          # real git dir (objects, refs, index, config)
.notes/                      # its worktree; parent excludes it via info/exclude
```

The content dir contains **no `.git` file** — we never run plain `git init`
inside it, so the parent sees only plain excluded files (no gitlink, no
submodule confusion, no trace). The inner repo is reachable only through
the wrapper:

```bash
git nook <name> <any-git-args...>
# ≈ exec git --git-dir=<common-git-dir>/nook/<name>.git \
#            --work-tree=<dir> "$@"
```

So the daily surface is exactly git:

```bash
git nook notes status
git nook notes add --all
git nook notes commit -m "something"
git nook notes pull
git nook notes push
git nook notes log -p
```

`add` wires the inner repo's remote and refspecs to the custom ref (below),
so `push`/`pull`/`fetch` and branch-tracking output (`ahead 1`) work out of
the box. Local branches, stash, reflog, mergetool — all available;
publication is single-ref (the configured push refspec), which is the only
branch-shaped constraint.

Conflicts are plain git conflicts: markers in the working files, resolved
with familiar tooling. There is no unattended mode, so no policy machinery
is needed to keep markers off the published ref — the user commits the
resolution like in any repo.

## Decision history

Locked during brainstorming (2026-07-05):

1. One tool, generalized in place. No engine extraction.
2. Custom refs (`refs/nook/<owner>/<project>/<name>`) are the default
   published location; branches remain one config line away.
3. Migration is manual, this repo only.
4. The machine-local project index and data dir are dropped.

Locked during design review (2026-07-06):

5. Filtering authority is the channel dir's own `.gitignore` (native inner
   git behavior); the host repo's ignore machinery never applies.
6. Byte identity: `core.autocrlf=false` set in the inner repo config at
   creation; parent attributes unreachable by construction.
7. `add` refuses a dir containing files tracked in the parent's HEAD, and a
   dir nesting inside (or containing) another channel's dir.

Locked during the inner-repo pivot (2026-07-06):

8. Channels are hidden inner git repositories (the section above).
9. Passthrough command surface: `git nook <name> <git-args...>`. Channel
   names must not collide with subcommand names (reserved: `add`, `list`,
   `show`, `remove`, `help`, `version`) and must be a single
   `check-ref-format` path component.
10. **There is no `sync` command.** Users run `push`/`pull` themselves;
    anyone who wants one-shot behavior scripts
    `add -A && commit && pull && push` in their own alias. Consequently:
    - The inner repo carries a **real configured remote** (`origin`) with
      refspecs baked to the published ref — no target key in parent
      config, no dynamic URL resolution. URL changes are
      `git nook <name> remote set-url`.
    - **No merge policies.** No beads-aware code, no `require_real_br`;
      **`br` is not a dependency of the tool at all.** Beads is a
      documented use case (below), not a code path.
    - No `set` subcommand and no old `init`/`target`; channel tweaks are
      inner-repo git config, the standard way.
11. `remove` deletes the channel's parent-config entry and `info/exclude`
    line only; the content dir and inner repo are left in place with a
    printed hint for manual deletion (the inner repo may hold unpushed
    history — never destroyed silently).

Naming (2026-07-06):

12. **`git nook`** — binary `bin/git-nook`, invocable as `git nook`.
    Earlier working names `br-orphanage` (beads-specific) and
    `git-orphanage` (no longer uses orphan *branches*) both misdescribe
    the final tool. Collision-checked 2026-07-06: no `git-nook` on
    GitHub, npm, Homebrew, or PyPI; nearby "nook" projects are unrelated
    (an e-reader, a browser, a Rust niche-types crate). A nook is where
    you tuck things away in your own home — discreet, not encrypted —
    which matches the honest privacy model: hidden from *view*, not from
    *access*.

### Prior art (and what nook adds)

The standard community answers to "track files the host repo can't track"
are: a nested repo in an excluded subdir; a repo elsewhere plus symlinks;
the dotfiles bare-repo technique (`git --git-dir=X --work-tree=<host>`
behind a shell alias); or a git worktree on a dedicated branch. Nook's
architecture *is* the bare-repo technique — deliberately; it's the
strongest of the four — with these deltas:

- **Remote-side hiding, the novel part.** All four standard approaches
  still need a second, visible repository to push to, which is itself a
  trace. Nook publishes to hidden custom refs on an *existing* remote
  (even the host's own origin): no second repo, no branch listing, no UI
  footprint, same transport and credentials.
- **Scoped work-tree.** The dotfiles technique sets the whole host dir as
  work-tree and needs `status.showUntrackedFiles no`, blinding `status`
  to your own new files and making `clean` dangerous. Nook's work-tree is
  the channel dir, so `status`/`add -A`/`clean` behave with full fidelity.
- **History survives `git clean`.** Unlike a nested repo (whose `.git`
  lives in the host tree and dies with `clean -fdx`), nook's git-dir
  lives under the parent's `.git/`, which clean never touches.
- **Repeatable setup.** `git nook add` encodes the whole per-machine
  ritual (exclude entry, autocrlf, refspecs, tracking, refusals,
  bootstrap) that the alias approach leaves as a wiki page.

The remaining trades are per-channel choices, not tool limitations:

- **Access control**: refs on the host's origin are visible to anyone
  with read access (`ls-remote`) — nook hides from view, not from
  access. When that matters, set the channel's target to a private repo
  only you own: full access-control separation, host repo still
  traceless. Both postures are one `add` argument apart.
- **Host web UI/PRs/CI**: hidden refs get none. When browsability
  matters more than invisibility, `--ref refs/heads/...` publishes to a
  normal branch on the target today. (A friendlier `--orphan-branch`
  convenience flag is possible future sugar — see Out of scope.)

### Alternatives considered and rejected

- *Custom snapshot plumbing in the parent repo* (the original 2026-07-05
  design: temp-index snapshots, state refs, hand-rolled three-way walk,
  byte-identity rules): reimplemented git behaviors the inner repo gets
  for free, with no user-facing git tooling. Superseded.
- *A `sync` orchestrator with merge policies (`git`/`beads`)* (first
  inner-repo draft): reimplemented `pull`/`push` orchestration users
  already know; hard-coded one use case (beads) into the engine; dragged
  in `br` as a dependency and an auto-commit policy nobody asked for.
  Users script their own one-liner.
- *`.git` file in the content dir* (`git init --separate-git-dir`): the
  parent would see a gitlink; breaks the no-trace invariant.
- *Dynamic target resolution from parent config*: only existed to serve
  `sync`; with plain `push`/`pull`, a configured inner remote is the
  standard mechanism and self-documenting.
- *Conflict-marker avoidance policies (local-wins etc.)*: only needed for
  unattended sync; interactive git users resolve conflicts normally.
- *Names implying secrecy or crypto* (`secrit`, vault/lockbox-style):
  oversell — anyone with read access can `ls-remote` the refs. Names with
  existing collisions: `gitless` (an existing VCS), `git-secret`,
  `git-crypt`, `git-ghost`, `git-annex`, builtins `notes`/`stash`.

## Channels

Parent-repo git config holds exactly one key per channel — the wrapper's
name → worktree mapping (and `list`'s data source):

```
nook.<name>.dir           # repo-relative content dir
```

Everything else lives where git expects it, in the inner repo:

```
core.autocrlf=false                      # byte identity (decision 6)
remote.origin.url=<url>                  # from `add <target>`
remote.origin.fetch=+<published-ref>:refs/remotes/origin/main
remote.origin.push=refs/heads/main:<published-ref>
branch.main.remote=origin
branch.main.merge=<published-ref>
```

The published ref defaults to `refs/nook/<owner>/<project>/<name>` (tokens
derived from the parent's origin URL exactly as today), overridable at
`add` time via `--ref`. A value starting with `refs/` is used verbatim;
anything else is prefixed `refs/heads/` (branches stay one flag away for
restrictive hosts). The template resolves once, at `add` time, into the
refspecs above; changing it later is standard `git config` in the inner
repo.

The inner git-dir lives at
`$(git rev-parse --path-format=absolute --git-common-dir)/nook/<name>.git`
— one inner repo per clone. Using a channel from multiple linked worktrees
of the same clone is unsupported (single primary worktree assumed); the
wrapper resolves `--work-tree` from the current checkout's toplevel at
runtime and persists no worktree path.

## Command surface

```
git nook add <name> <target-url-or-remote> [--dir <d>] [--ref <tpl>]
git nook list                     # channels in this project
git nook show <name>              # resolved dir/url/ref, branch state
git nook remove <name>            # per decision 11
git nook <name> <git-args...>     # full git against the inner repo
git nook --help | --version
```

`add`:
- Refuses reserved/invalid names, a dir containing files tracked in the
  parent's HEAD, and a dir nesting inside (or containing) another
  channel's dir (decisions 7/9).
- `--dir` defaults to `.<name>/`. Ensures the dir exists and adds it to
  the parent's `.git/info/exclude` (worktree-safe via `--git-path`, as
  today).
- `<target>` may be a URL or the name of a parent remote (resolved to its
  URL once, at add time).
- Creates the inner repo on branch `main` (explicitly, ignoring
  `init.defaultBranch`) with the config block above.
- If the published ref already exists on the target (fresh clone / new
  machine), finishes with the git-native bootstrap: `fetch` +
  `reset --hard origin/main`. If both local files and a remote ref exist,
  it stops and tells the user to reconcile with
  `pull --allow-unrelated-histories` after committing — never destructive.

`show` is a convenience read: dir, remote URL, push refspec, plus
`status -sb`-style branch/tracking state from the inner repo.

## Beads: a use case, not a feature

This repo's own issue tracking uses a `beads` nook (`--dir .beads`). The
workflow, encoded in AGENTS.md (session protocol) rather than in the tool:

```bash
br sync --flush-only                       # DB → JSONL
git nook beads add -A
git nook beads commit -m "issues"
git nook beads pull                        # if divergent: see below
git nook beads push
```

Notes carried over from the current tool's empirical findings (br 0.2.16,
docs/empirical-findings.md):

- br's own `.beads/.gitignore` already excludes all local state
  (`*.db*`, `*.lock`, `.br_history/`, `last-touched`, daemon files, ...) —
  the inner repo honors it natively. `.gitignore` itself gets one line in
  the inner repo's `info/exclude` (br regenerates it; syncing it invites
  cross-version churn). This is part of the documented setup, not tool
  code.
- On a divergent pull, a plain text merge of `issues.jsonl` is usually
  wrong; the documented procedure is the validated br dance: take theirs,
  `br sync --import-only`, `br sync --flush-only --force`, then adopt
  remote bytes verbatim when the import reported no changes and the id
  sets match (br's serialization is not byte-stable across machines;
  skipping adoption can ping-pong bytes between machines). This lives as a
  small helper script committed on the *beads nook itself* (dogfood:
  shadow-tracked, invisible on master), referenced from AGENTS.md.
- Fresh-machine setup: `git nook add beads origin --dir .beads`
  (bootstraps from the remote ref), then `br init` if the local br
  workspace files are absent, then `br sync --import-only`.

## Repo rename (gh)

The GitHub repo is `happycollision/br-orphanage`; both prior names
misdescribe the tool. Rename early — before migration — so
`<owner>/<project>` tokens derive from the final name
(`refs/nook/happycollision/git-nook/beads`). GitHub redirects the old URL,
but update everything anyway:

```bash
# From inside the repo — renames on GitHub AND updates the local
# origin remote URL in one step:
gh repo rename git-nook

# Verify:
git remote get-url origin          # git@github.com:happycollision/git-nook.git
git ls-remote origin HEAD

# The current beads sync target is the remote NAME 'origin'
# (beadsOrphanage.target=origin), so it tracks the URL change with
# nothing to do until migration.

# Other machines/clones (redirect would work; be explicit anyway):
git remote set-url origin git@github.com:happycollision/git-nook.git

# Optional cosmetic, per machine:
mv ~/GitProjects/git-orphanage ~/GitProjects/git-nook
```

This supersedes open issue beads-sync-gfv (rename local checkout dir) —
close it with a pointer here during migration.

## Migration (manual, this repo only)

The old published branch (`orphanage/happycollision/br-orphanage` on
origin) is a chain of snapshot commits the inner repo adopts directly as
its history. Prerequisite: the repo rename above is done.

0. Upgrade the installed binary on every machine/clone FIRST — an old
   `br-orphanage sync` run after step 5 would recreate the deleted branch.
1. `git nook add beads origin --dir .beads`. `.beads/` already exists and
   the new ref doesn't, so this is the plain-add path.
2. In the inner repo: `git nook beads fetch origin
   refs/heads/orphanage/happycollision/br-orphanage` and
   `git nook beads reset --hard FETCH_HEAD` — full history preserved. Add
   `.gitignore` to the inner repo's `info/exclude`.
3. Delete the `beadsOrphanage.*` keys and the old local state refs
   (`refs/orphanage/pushed`, `refs/orphanage/fetched`).
4. `git nook beads push` — publishes the adopted history to
   `refs/nook/happycollision/git-nook/beads`.
5. Delete the old branch from the target
   (`git push origin :refs/heads/orphanage/happycollision/br-orphanage`).
6. Update AGENTS.md/CLAUDE.md to the new session protocol; commit the
   beads-merge helper script to the beads nook.
7. Verify: `git nook beads status` clean and up to date;
   `git nook beads log` shows the old snapshot history;
   `git ls-remote origin` shows the new ref and no old branch.

The tool itself simply errors on unknown channels, with a hint pointing at
`git nook add` — no legacy-key detection.

## Errors, safety, style

- The wrapper adds no error handling around passthrough git — git's own
  messages are the UX. Wrapper-owned commands (`add`, `list`, `show`,
  `remove`) check every step, as today.
- Non-fast-forward push rejection (git-native) is the cross-machine
  concurrency story: fetch/pull, reconcile, push again.
- bash + git only, `set -euo pipefail`, shellcheck-clean. No dependency on
  `br` or anything else.

## Renaming & docs impact

- `bin/br-orphanage` → `bin/git-nook`; `VERSION` → `0.2.0`; message
  prefixes → `git-nook:`.
- `install.sh` installs `git-nook` only; uninstall notes for the old
  binary name.
- README rewritten around "git for a hidden directory": the passthrough
  surface, nook setup, and the beads use case as a worked example.
- AGENTS.md/CLAUDE.md session protocol rewritten (see Beads section).
- `~/.local/share/br-orphanage` data dir is gone — the tool keeps zero
  machine-global state.
- GitHub repo rename per the section above.

## Planning process for this effort

Per the AGENTS.md branch override: designs and implementation plans for
this rework are plain markdown under `docs/plans/`, committed on this
working branch — NOT beads issues (the tool that publishes the beads nook
is the thing being rebuilt). Before merging to master, the plan/design
commits are removed from history (rebase/squash to a clean final series).
Beads-based planning resumes once `git nook` is stable and migrated.

## Testing (tests/run.sh)

Rewrite the end-to-end suite around the new surface:

- Passthrough round trip: `<name> status`/`add`/`commit`/`log`; no `.git`
  entry ever appears in the content dir; parent `git status` stays clean
  throughout.
- `push` publishes to the custom ref (verified via `ls-remote`); `pull` on
  a second clone materializes content; branch-tracking state (`ahead`/
  `behind`) reports correctly through the shim.
- Non-fast-forward push rejected; fetch + merge + push succeeds after.
- Conflicting edits from two clones: `pull` yields real conflict markers;
  resolve + commit + push round-trips.
- `add` bootstrap path: fresh clone with existing remote ref materializes
  the dir; both-sides-exist case refuses with the reconcile hint.
- Channel-dir `.gitignore` honored; a pattern in the PARENT's
  `info/exclude`/global excludes demonstrably does NOT apply.
- Byte identity: CRLF file round-trips exactly with `core.autocrlf=true`
  in the parent and global config.
- Ref override via `--ref` (publishes under `refs/heads/...`).
- `add` refusals: reserved name, invalid ref component, parent-tracked
  files in dir, overlapping dirs.
- `remove` then re-`add` of the same name; inner-repo hint printed.
- `list`/`show` output sanity.

(The manual migration checklist and repo rename are executed, not tested.)

## Out of scope (YAGNI)

- Any `sync`/orchestration command (decision 10); users alias their own.
- Merge policies, beads-aware code paths, any `br` dependency.
- Multi-ref publication per channel (local branches work; the push
  refspec publishes `main` only).
- An `--orphan-branch` convenience flag (sugar over `--ref refs/heads/...`
  for the browsable-branch posture; explicitly future work, not now).
- Discovery of existing nook refs on a target (beads-sync-lp0).
- Encryption/private content transforms; non-git storage backends.
- Any shell interception (settled by the standalone-only epic).
close_reason: Shipped 2026-07-06: built on branch git-nook, beads migrated to refs/nook, merged to master. This epic + its child are the durable design/plan record (markdown removed from repo history).

title: Implementation plan: git-nook rewrite (executed 2026-07-06)
description:
# git-nook Implementation Plan > **For agentic workers:** REQUIRED SUB-SKILL: Use superpowers:subagent-driven-development (recommended) or superpowers:executing-plans to implement this plan task-by-task. Steps use checkbox (`- [ ]`) syntax for tracking. **Goal:** Replace `bin/br-orphanage` with `bin/git-nook` — a channel creator plus git shim that gives real git tracking to a directory the parent repo cannot see, publishing to hidden custom refs. **Architecture:** Each "nook" is an ordinary git repository whose git-dir lives at `/.git/nook/.git` and whose worktree is an excluded content dir (no `.git` file inside it). `git nook add` creates and wires it (remote + refspecs to a custom ref); `git nook ` execs git against it; everything else IS git. Spec: `docs/plans/2026-07-05-git-nook-design.md`. **Tech Stack:** bash (`set -euo pipefail`, shellcheck-clean), git only — no `br` dependency. End-to-end tests in `tests/run.sh` (self-contained mktemp harness, no real HOME/remotes). **Conventions for every task:** - Run the whole suite with `tests/run.sh` (from anywhere). It must exit 0 at every commit. - New test sections are inserted in `tests/run.sh` **immediately before** the `# --- shellcheck` section, unless a task says otherwise. - Keep `shellcheck bin/git-nook install.sh tests/run.sh` clean. Use targeted `# shellcheck disable=SCnnnn` comments with a reason, matching existing style, ONLY when the warning is a false positive. - Work directly on the `git-nook` branch. Commit after each task; do not push (the supervisor pushes at checkpoints). --- ### Task 1: New skeleton + new test harness (clean slate) **Files:** - Create: `bin/git-nook` (mode 755) - Delete: `bin/br-orphanage` - Rewrite: `tests/run.sh` (mode 755) - [ ] **Step 1: Rewrite `tests/run.sh` with the new harness and first sections** Replace the entire file with: ```bash #!/usr/bin/env bash set -euo pipefail # tests/run.sh — end-to-end harness for git-nook. # # Everything runs inside a throwaway `mktemp -d`, cleaned up on exit via # trap. Nothing touches the invoking user's real home directory, git # config, or any real remote. git-nook depends only on git — no br needed. # # Run: tests/run.sh (from anywhere; resolves its own path) SELF=$(readlink -f "${BASH_SOURCE[0]}") TESTS_DIR=$(cd "$(dirname "${SELF}")" && pwd) REPO_UNDER_TEST=$(cd "${TESTS_DIR}/.." && pwd) NOOK="${REPO_UNDER_TEST}/bin/git-nook" WORK=$(mktemp -d "${TMPDIR:-/tmp}/git-nook-test.XXXXXX") # shellcheck disable=SC2329 # invoked indirectly via the EXIT trap below cleanup() { local status=$? rm -rf "${WORK}" exit "${status}" } trap cleanup EXIT # Fake HOME + no system config: the user's real gitconfig (aliases, # autocrlf, excludesFile) must never leak into test behavior. FAKE_HOME="${WORK}/home" mkdir -p "${FAKE_HOME}" export HOME="${FAKE_HOME}" export XDG_DATA_HOME="${FAKE_HOME}/.local/share" export XDG_CONFIG_HOME="${FAKE_HOME}/.config" export GIT_CONFIG_NOSYSTEM=1 export GIT_AUTHOR_NAME=test GIT_AUTHOR_EMAIL=test@test.invalid export GIT_COMMITTER_NAME=test GIT_COMMITTER_EMAIL=test@test.invalid # --- Pass/fail bookkeeping ----------------------------------------------------- PASS_COUNT=0 FAIL_COUNT=0 section() { printf '\n=== %s ===\n' "$1"; } pass() { PASS_COUNT=$((PASS_COUNT + 1)) printf ' [PASS] %s\n' "$1" } fail() { FAIL_COUNT=$((FAIL_COUNT + 1)) printf ' [FAIL] %s\n' "$1" >&2 } assert_eq() { local desc="$1" expected="$2" actual="$3" if [[ "${expected}" == "${actual}" ]]; then pass "${desc}" else fail "${desc} (expected: '${expected}', actual: '${actual}')" fi } assert_true() { local desc="$1" shift if "$@"; then pass "${desc}" else fail "${desc} (command failed: $*)" fi } assert_file_exists() { local desc="$1" path="$2" if [[ -f "${path}" ]]; then pass "${desc}"; else fail "${desc} (missing file: ${path})"; fi } assert_file_absent() { local desc="$1" path="$2" if [[ ! -e "${path}" ]]; then pass "${desc}"; else fail "${desc} (file unexpectedly present: ${path})"; fi } assert_dir_exists() { local desc="$1" path="$2" if [[ -d "${path}" ]]; then pass "${desc}"; else fail "${desc} (missing directory: ${path})"; fi } assert_contains() { local desc="$1" haystack="$2" needle="$3" if [[ "${haystack}" == *"${needle}"* ]]; then pass "${desc}" else fail "${desc} (did not find '${needle}' in output; got: '${haystack}')" fi } # Run a command expected to FAIL; captures RUN_OUT / RUN_EXIT for assertions. RUN_OUT="" RUN_EXIT=0 run_cmd() { set +e RUN_OUT=$("$@" 2>&1) RUN_EXIT=$? set -e } # Same, but run from a directory (macOS env has no -C; use a subshell cd). run_cmd_in() { local dir="$1" shift set +e RUN_OUT=$(cd "${dir}" && "$@" 2>&1) RUN_EXIT=$? set -e } assert_exit_nonzero() { local desc="$1" if [[ "${RUN_EXIT}" -ne 0 ]]; then pass "${desc} (${RUN_EXIT})" else fail "${desc} (unexpectedly exited 0; output: '${RUN_OUT}')" fi } # --- Shared helpers ------------------------------------------------------------- # `git rev-parse --git-path

` is relative from a normal repo but absolute # from a linked worktree. title: CI release workflow + versioned installs
description:
Add CI (test on push/PR), a tag-triggered release workflow, and versioned installs. Master/local-checkout builds print 'git-nook post-vX.Y.Z-dev'; tagged releases print 'git-nook vX.Y.Z'.

CORE MECHANIC: A committed VERSION file (repo root) holds the bare LAST-RELEASED number, e.g. 0.2.0. bin/git-nook line 22 becomes a placeholder VERSION="v0.0.0-dev". The real version is stamped into a COPY of the script at build/install time via a shared scripts/stamp-version.sh helper — committed source is never mutated. The existing 'echo "git-nook ${VERSION}"' needs no logic change; it echoes whatever was stamped.

RESOLUTION TABLE:
- Local checkout install (master): stamped 'post-v0.2.0-dev' -> prints 'git-nook post-v0.2.0-dev'
- Tagged release v0.2.0: stamped 'v0.2.0' -> prints 'git-nook v0.2.0'
- Raw untouched source: placeholder 'v0.0.0-dev' -> prints 'git-nook v0.0.0-dev'

VERSION trails one release behind master: after releasing v0.2.0, file says 0.2.0 and master reports 'post-v0.2.0-dev' (building on top of v0.2.0) until next release bumps it.

Full design spec (working copy, untracked, NOT committed to master): docs/superpowers/specs/2026-07-09-ci-release-versioning-design.md

Children are chained with blocks deps so 'br ready' surfaces one step at a time in order.
close_reason: CI + tag-triggered releases + versioned installs shipped. All 8 children done, subagent-driven with 2-stage review. PR #1 open, CI green (fixed SC2317 trap false-positive found by running CI on its own PR). 165/165 tests.

2. EDIT: bin/git-nook line 22. The comment MUST be on its OWN line ABOVE the assignment (a trailing inline comment on the VERSION= line breaks the anchored regex ^VERSION="\(.*\)"$ used by install.sh tool_version() and tests/run.sh, silently yielding an empty version). Result:
     # VERSION is replaced at build/install time; the VERSION file is the source of truth.
     VERSION="v0.0.0-dev"

DO NOT touch the --version/version case (~line 430) or usage().

VERIFY: sed -n 's/^VERSION="\(.*\)"$/\1/p' bin/git-nook prints v0.0.0-dev (non-empty); raw bin/git-nook --version prints 'git-nook v0.0.0-dev'.

Line endings: repo pins LF via .gitattributes.

title: Add scripts/stamp-version.sh shared helper
description:
STEP 2 of the CI/release epic (br-orphanage-ee8). Single stamp implementation, called by install.sh, release.yml, and tests — no duplication.

NEW FILE: scripts/stamp-version.sh (chmod +x):

  #!/usr/bin/env bash
  set -euo pipefail
  # stamp-version.sh <target-file> <version-string>
  # Rewrites the VERSION="..." line of <target-file> in place.
  target="$1"
  version="$2"
  sed -i.bak "s|^VERSION=.*|VERSION=\"${version}\"|" "${target}"
  rm -f "${target}.bak"

NOTES:
- sed -i.bak for BSD(macOS)/GNU portability (matches repo convention; local dev is BSD sed).
- '|' delimiter so version strings never need escaping.
- ^VERSION= anchor matches exactly one line in bin/git-nook.

Must pass shellcheck (repo is shellcheck-clean).

title: Add .github/workflows/release.yml
description:
STEP 6 of the CI/release epic (br-orphanage-ee8). Tag-triggered: guard -> test -> stamp -> checksum -> release.

NEW FILE .github/workflows/release.yml:

  name: Release
  on:
    push:
      tags: ['v*']
  permissions:
    contents: write
  jobs:
    release:
      runs-on: ubuntu-latest
      steps:
        - uses: actions/checkout@v4
        - name: Guard — tag must equal v<VERSION>
          run: scripts/check-tag-version.sh "${GITHUB_REF_NAME}"
        - name: shellcheck
          run: shellcheck bin/git-nook install.sh tests/run.sh scripts/stamp-version.sh scripts/check-tag-version.sh
        - name: tests
          run: tests/run.sh
        - name: Build stamped artifact
          run: |
            mkdir -p dist
            cp bin/git-nook dist/git-nook
            scripts/stamp-version.sh dist/git-nook "v$(cat VERSION)"
            chmod +x dist/git-nook
            ( cd dist && sha256sum git-nook > git-nook.sha256 )
        - name: Create GitHub Release
          uses: softprops/action-gh-release@v2
          with:
            files: |
              dist/git-nook
              dist/git-nook.sha256

The guard now calls scripts/check-tag-version.sh (ee8.8) — the SAME script tests exercise, not inline YAML.
CHECKSUM: 'cd dist' first so it's recorded by BASENAME (git-nook) — what install.sh verify_checksum compares. After release, releases/latest/download/git-nook{,.sha256} resolve, matching install.sh default RELEASE_BASE.

title: Document the release procedure
description:
STEP 7 of the CI/release epic (br-orphanage-ee8). Document the human release steps (README.md or AGENTS.md — pick where install/dev docs live).

RELEASE PROCEDURE:
1. Bump VERSION file to the new number (e.g. 0.3.0), commit on master.
2. git tag v0.3.0 && git push origin v0.3.0
3. release.yml guards (tag == v$(cat VERSION)), tests, stamps clean, checksums, publishes the GitHub Release. install.sh (default RELEASE_BASE=releases/latest) then serves it.
4. Master builds now report 'post-v0.3.0-dev'.

Also note: VERSION holds the LAST-RELEASED number; master reports post-v<that>-dev.

title: Add scripts/check-tag-version.sh (real, testable tag guard)
description:
Extract the tag-vs-VERSION guard into a real script so it is testable and reusable by release.yml (same DRY move as stamp-version.sh). Without this, the guard lives only as inline YAML and any test of it is vacuous (comparing VERSION to itself).

NEW FILE scripts/check-tag-version.sh (chmod +x):

  #!/usr/bin/env bash
  set -euo pipefail
  # check-tag-version.sh <tag> [version-file]
  # Exits 0 iff <tag> equals "v<contents-of-version-file>". Default file: VERSION.
  if [[ $# -lt 1 || $# -gt 2 ]]; then
      echo "usage: check-tag-version.sh <tag> [version-file]" >&2
      exit 2
  fi
  tag="$1"
  version_file="${2:-VERSION}"
  expected="v$(cat "${version_file}")"
  if [[ "${tag}" != "${expected}" ]]; then
      echo "check-tag-version.sh: tag ${tag} != ${expected} (from ${version_file})" >&2
      exit 1
  fi

Exit codes: 0 match, 1 mismatch, 2 usage error. Optional version-file arg lets tests point at a fixture. Must pass shellcheck. Do not modify other files.

Origin: Task 4 review found the inline tag-guard test was vacuous; extracting a real script fixes it. release.yml (Task 6) calls: scripts/check-tag-version.sh "${GITHUB_REF_NAME}".

title: Nook content dir resolves per-toplevel, breaking use from linked worktrees
description:
Not beads-specific — br/beads is just the concrete case. This is a git-nook design gap with linked worktrees (git worktree add) of the host repo.

Concrete case (2026-07-10, TrilliantHealth/engineering-frontend): ran `git nook add beads origin` from the main tree at ~/GitProjects/engineering-frontend, then `br init`. Works perfectly there. But daily work happens in linked worktrees under ~/GitProjects/engineering-frontend.worktrees/<branch>/ (siblings of the main tree, not nested inside it). From any of those worktrees:

1. `git nook beads status` fails: "nook 'beads' has no content dir at <worktree-toplevel>/.beads (was it deleted?)". Nothing was deleted — the content dir only exists in the main tree.
2. `git nook list` from the same worktree happily lists the nook as if usable (config is shared via the common git dir), so the failure reads as corruption rather than by-design.
3. The error hint is actively dangerous: it suggests mkdir -p plus `git nook beads checkout -- .`, which would create a SECOND checkout of the SAME inner repo (one shared index/HEAD under .git/nook/beads.git, two content dirs). Edits in one tree silently stale the other's copy, and status/add results flip depending on where you run.
4. Downstream, br cannot auto-discover .beads/*.db from the worktree (sibling path, not a child of the main tree), so every br invocation there needs --db pointing at the main tree.

Why it happens: the inner git-dir (common_git_dir()/nook/<name>.git), the nook.<name>.dir config, and the info/exclude entry are all resolved via the COMMON git dir — deliberately worktree-safe. The content dir is the one piece that is not: cmd_add creates ${dir} relative to `git rev-parse --show-toplevel` of whatever tree it runs in, and run_passthrough computes worktree="${toplevel}/${dir}" from the CURRENT toplevel. So a nook added in the main tree half-exists in every linked worktree, and a nook added FROM a worktree plants its content dir in a throwaway tree while polluting the shared config/exclude.

Workaround that works today: only run git-nook (and br) from the main worktree.

Workaround that ALMOST works: symlink .beads in each worktree -> the main tree's .beads. run_passthrough already resolves physically (cd + pwd -P), so passthrough functions through the symlink, and br follows it too. But the exclude entry written by add is "/${dir}/" — the trailing slash matches directories only, so the symlink itself shows as untracked in every worktree.

Directions to consider (needs brainstorming, not prescribing):
- Resolve the content dir against the MAIN worktree's toplevel regardless of where the command runs (e.g. first entry of `git worktree list --porcelain`), so one canonical content dir serves all worktrees. This matches the semantics implied by the shared inner repo/index.
- Or record an absolute anchor path in nook.<name>.* config at add time.
- Or have add/passthrough auto-manage per-worktree symlinks (and write the exclude entry without the trailing slash so the symlink is covered).
- At minimum: make run_passthrough's error distinguish "you are in a linked worktree; the content dir lives at <path>" from actual deletion, and stop suggesting the mkdir+checkout footgun; possibly have `git nook list` flag nooks whose content dir is absent at this toplevel.
- Watch out: `git nook add` run from a linked worktree should probably refuse or anchor to the main tree; unusual --git-common-dir layouts need care.
